speaker: interviewer
text: --on the boat. Although before you got on the boats, you did see someone very, very infamous while you were waiting for the boat in Genoa.

speaker: interviewee
text: While I was in Genoa, I roamed around. And I happened to get to some kind of an official function, where I got a glimpse of Mussolini and King Victor Emmanuel. I had my camera with me. I took a shot of the two of them-- not at them, of them. And the picture-- I brought the picture home. Then I-- from Genoa, I finally got on the boat. It was a rather uneventful trip to Cuba, with a brief side trip to Seville, where a member of the-- of the surveillance group of the Spanish Civil War joined the boat. They were supervising to make sure that there are no arms shipments coming in or out. We finally landed in Cuba on the 15th of January, 1939. There was a brief scare. We all got called to the captain-- very closely checked our documents. But it was all right. And my brother had arranged for somebody in Cuba to pick me up from the boat, got me into a local hotel for a few days. And then we found a room by a local Cuban family. I joined the local Jewish community on the basis of-- that they had facilities. They gave Spanish courses. I learned Spanish. There was an English teacher there who gave English courses. I learned some English. And of course, we were not allowed to work in Cuba at all. But we had founded a Jewish Refugee Committee, a social club, so to speak. We borrowed some records from local people. We put on music evenings for the refugees, who were started for any kind of culture. And we lived on until a ship from Germany came in, the famous which is known as the 'ship of the damned.' We immediately found out that they were denied landing although they had paid for landing permits. There were some intrigues. You can read it in the book. I have it. And so we started a big campaign sending telegrams to anyone in the United States we could imagine, from President Roosevelt to Morgenthau to Ickes to the-- Hull and the Joint and all over. And I was one who spoke reasonably English already. And so I served as translator and transporter for the-- for the telegrams. And, of course, we had to collect money from the local Jewish community and from the local Jewish merchants and the local refugees. As it came out, it didn't help. And after five days, we came down to the harbor in the morning. And the was gone. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: After that-- Let me ask you a question about the Did you speak to any of the passengers? Did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, we could not get there. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Only close relatives managed to hire small boats in the harbor. And they were allowed to go within yelling distance because police boats were circling all the time.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was this, exactly?

speaker: interviewee
text: The date? I--

speaker: interviewer
text: Or the month and the year. I think we should just clarify that for people in the future who will watch the tape.

speaker: interviewee
text: I do not know the exact date. But I have read in the book. I can look it up.

speaker: interviewer
text: Wasn't it-- wasn't it--

speaker: interviewee
text: May, I believe.

speaker: interviewer
text: Of-- but what year?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was 1939, yes. I believe it was middle May, 1939. And after they were gone, life went on in Cuba. There were demonstrations occasionally of Cuban Nazis because the German Embassy had started making trouble there on the order, as we found out later from the book, on the order of the German government. The German Embassy, they denied me an extension of my passport. And I had to get to the American Embassy to get an extension of my German passport. It's all in my passport. Anyway, and finally at the end of October, my permit came. It was posted at the door of the embassy where periodically at the beginning of every month, the ones were posted who had permit, whose visa had become active to go to the United States. I took the boat train from Havana to Key West, got on the Greyhound in Key West, and had a very comfortable seat in the front row. Behind me was a well-dressed American couple who spoke very subduedly, except occasionally their voices raised up.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then I heard things, who need those hooked noses here? They shouldn't let them in. They should change them back where they come from. And then conversation went on a even level again.

speaker: interviewee
text: American WASPs who did not like-- I was obviously a refugee. I wore European clothes, whatever thing. I talked to somebody and had heavy Austrian accent. They knew-- damn Jew. And they let me know it. So that was my greeting in the United States.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when you should have felt relief, you probably--

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. I was a bit depressed. But I got then-- from Key West, I came to Miami. Yeah, but they-- a customs officer in Key West was exceedingly nice. He welcomed me, wished me luck. He realized why I am coming, wished me good luck, and expressed hope that the United States would be salvation and haven for me. So that was nice. Anyway, I finally-- the trip took until Sunday afternoon-- from Friday morning to Sunday afternoon. I got to New York. At the terminal, there was my fiance, her cousin who had gotten her in, my brother and his wife, who had gotten me in. And we all-- I had hoped to be able to wash up and change. I hadn't shaved in three days. And my cousin insisted on taking me to lunch right away. And we went-- if you know New York, we went to Linda's, of all the people-- of all the places. I was very uncomfortable in my garb and unwashed. [LAUGHS] But anyway, we had lunch. And finally at night, I got into a room my father had rented. I-- the next day, I immediately applied for first papers. I spoke enough English to be let out in New York. I applied for my first papers. And on-- that was on Monday. My fiance had to go back to work. She worked in Brooklyn. And the same Friday, I got a job offer for unskilled labor in a small place in New York. I accepted. And for three months, I worked in that place.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was it called?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was Elk Tools, E-L-K Tools, Incorporated.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when did you get married?

speaker: interviewee
text: We got married three weeks after I came to-- after I landed in New York.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the date of your marriage?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Sunday was the 19th of November, 1939.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when did your parents arrive in the United--

speaker: interviewee
text: My parents arrived here on the 29th of February, 1940.

speaker: interviewer
text: How-- who-- were you and your brother responsible for getting them to America?

speaker: interviewee
text: My brother was. I had nothing to do with it because I only had come a few months before.

speaker: interviewer
text: So your whole family was reunited?

speaker: interviewee
text: Our whole family was reunited, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how many children did you and Adele have?

speaker: interviewee
text: Adele and I had two, the older girl, born on Washington's birthday in 1943.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Katherine Rose, after our two mothers. That's customary. And the boy came on September 6, 1946.

speaker: interviewer
text: And his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: His name is Roger Frank. And he is-- they're both independent. They live their own lifes. They both have family. And it might be interesting-- Kati is working in her own place as condominium management company. My son is a minister in a small, fundamental Protestant Church in Alaska.

speaker: interviewer
text: I just have a couple more questions, and this is regarding-- how long did your parents live?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother, unfortunately, died in November, 1939-- oh, 1940, excuse me. And what we believed was a duodenal cancer, but the diagnosis was a little different. It was a horrible time. At the funeral, my wife caught a cold, turned into TB, and so on. But that's her story. After three months, the job was over. I got another and then another as unskilled labor. But in November, 1940, I finally got a permanent job as a junior draftsman at a company in New Jersey, Konmar Products Corporation, the second biggest zipper company in the United States and the whole world.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what about your father? How long did he live?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father left until age 96.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which was what year?

speaker: interviewee
text: Which was 1970.

speaker: interviewer
text: My goodness.

speaker: interviewee
text: He was born 1874. And he got remarried in 1988.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: How could you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, excuse me, at age 88. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he'd been a widower for a long time.

speaker: interviewee
text: For a long time. But he wasn't alone.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: He had met that woman through the the Jewish newspaper in New York, the refugee newspaper. And they had lived together for a while. But that-- when he was 88, he decided, I'll give her my name, too--

speaker: interviewer
text: And when--

speaker: interviewee
text: --typical of my dad.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when did you come to California? What year?

speaker: interviewee
text: To California, I came June-- July 15, 1957.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you continued to work in your chosen profession?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- yes. In-- once I got into my profession in New York, I was able to stay with it, first in New York, New Jersey, then, for a brief interlude in North Carolina, and then here in Pasadena, California. I worked only for two companies until I retired.

speaker: interviewer
text: And now that it's over 50 years since the war is over, and 57 years since you made that voyage from Austria, what is it that you want to pass on to your children and your grandchildren about what happened to you in Austria? What do you want them to remember? What do you want them to remember as the most-- what's your legacy?

speaker: interviewee
text: The most important thing is that you can't get away from being a Jew. But they are not affected by it. Because none of them, except my daughter, are Jewish anymore-- religious Jewish anymore. My son still [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] always speaks about it. But he also has become baptized. Otherwise, I want them to know that they have to behave and work. They have been brought up the European way, disciplined and well-behaved and respectful. I wrote my story in several-- about 150 pages, put all the things down which I have, to extent-- to an certain extent told you now. And they have copies of it. Several people around me, my surrounding friends, relatives, have copies of the book. And the main thing-- the kids all love me. I love them. And that's about as much as you can hope for. They loved my wife. My wife was a wonderful mother and a wonderful grandmother. She volunteered for years in a Huntington Hospital in the-- in the premature baby section. That was her life. And otherwise, I now work as much as possible as a volunteer in the Pacific Asia museum in Pasadena. We just had a visit of-- from the Dalai Lama in Pasadena. And we had put on a big affair, mandala sand painting in our museum. And we had tremendous crowds, 400, 500 people coming in. And I was quite active in that event.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what about your wife, Adele? When did-- is she--

speaker: interviewee
text: She died-- she died in 1985. And I have been alone ever since. And let me tell you, it ain't funny. To be alone after 46 years of marriage is very difficult. But I had a choice-- live or don't live. And I decided, I'll make a go of it. I'll live. And I think I succeeded.

speaker: interviewer
text: It certainly seems that way.

speaker: interviewee
text: I think you have proof now.

speaker: interviewer
text: [LAUGHS] I think so.

speaker: interviewee
text: I have a nice home. This is a little bit too big for me. I'm rattling around here, but it was perfect for us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, I think it's lovely.

speaker: interviewee
text: You haven't seen the rest of it. But that's-- I'm-- that's not important. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, what I've seen is nice. But we do have some photographs which we're going to film next.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: But I want to take this opportunity to thank you very much for sharing so much of your life with us.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Thank you for the interview. I'm flattered.

speaker: interviewer
text: [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: And my friends wanted to know, how do I get that on there? How do I [? rate ?] it? Who gave you the information? How did you come to me?

speaker: interviewee
text: How did you come to me? How did the Shoah come to me?

speaker: interviewer
text: I don't know.

speaker: interviewee
text: You don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you call the Shoah? We can stop the tape.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture of my father, Sigfried Schneider, who was in the Communication Corps of the Austrian Army. The picture was taken at the [? Zaufin ?] Alpe, which is a little restaurant in the surrounding of Vienna. He is there, sitting in front, the only one with three stars on the lapel-- on the collar of his uniform.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is he on the right-- on your right or on your left?

speaker: interviewee
text: He is on the left side of the table.

speaker: interviewer
text: Without the hat? Is see in the front? Without--

speaker: interviewee
text: Without the hat, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Without the hat. And what year was this photo taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: That photo what was taken in September, 1918. This is a picture of my mother, Rosa Schneider, taken in 1915 during the war and was sent to my father. On the left side of my mother, it's myself. On the right side is my brother, Ernst. On the right is me, Otto. This is a picture of the 25th silver anniversary of my parents. In front is my father, my mother. Behind my mother is my--

speaker: interviewer
text: No, no, let's start again. Tell us-- you need to tell us your mother's full name, your father's full name--

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: --and everybody's full names.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. In front is my father, Sigfried Schneider, next to him, my mother, Rosa Schneider. Behind my mother is my brother, Ernst Schneider. And behind my father, is myself, Otto Schneider.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was this photograph taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was taken in 1932.

speaker: interviewer
text: And in what city and country?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Vienna, Austria. This picture was taken during a trip to Budapest, which I took in 1932, and which was my first experience in a foreign country with the swastika. I do not know what 'Eleg volt' means, but it was revealing for me that they had gotten to Budapest already. In 1931, I was a member of the Republikanischer Schutzbund. That was the paramilitary organization of the Austrian Social Democratic Party. That was during a training march. And I am the one who is carrying the standard in front of the entire group. This is a picture taken in September, 1938, in Vienna, Austria, showing my then girlfriend, later my wife, Adele, with myself--

speaker: interviewer
text: Otto.

speaker: interviewee
text: Otto.

speaker: interviewer
text: You should give us her last name at the time.

speaker: interviewee
text: What? Oh, at that time, my, my girlfriend's name was Adele [? Spurling. ?] This picture shows the outside of our store and little manufacturing outfit in the Stumpergasse, in Vienna, Austria. It was taken in 1940. And the name is shown in Latin letters and in Hebrew letters as it was at that time required for all Jewish businesses. Originally, that information was stretching over the entire front. But that-- by decree of the Nazis, we had to reduce it to the side of that one door.

speaker: interviewer
text: Just tell us what city and country was this in.

speaker: interviewee
text: I, I believe I said that-- in Vienna, Austria.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was the name of your family's business?

speaker: interviewee
text: The, the business was called Sigfried Schneider, [SPEAKING GERMAN], which means manufacturing of underwear. Yeah. This shows a picture which I was able to take while uninvited attending a [INAUDIBLE] review in Genoa in December, 1938. In the middle in front, in the light uniform there, is Benito Mussolini. And towards the middle of the picture, partially hidden by another head, is King Victor Emmanuel, then king of Italy.

speaker: interviewee
text: This shows myself, Otto Schneider, in Havana, Cuba, while I was waiting for my entry visa to the United States. It was taken in-- oh, when was it? In 1939.

speaker: interviewee
text: In-- sometimes in 1939. This picture was taken in New York, New York, in the apartment of my brother. It shows the four Schneider generations. Going from right to left is my son, Roger Frank Snyder; myself, Otto Snyder; my dad, at that time, 88 years old, Sigfried Schneider; and finally, my older brother, Ernst Snyder, on the far left.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what year was this photograph taken? 1956, I think.

speaker: interviewee
text: In-- no, no, no, no, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: It says on the back 1956. Doesn't it? Yeah. It says on the back--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, the picture was taken in 1956. This is my favorite picture showing my wife, Adele Snyder, and myself, Otto Snyder, in a loving embrace in 1984.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where-- what city--

speaker: interviewee
text: It was taken in Pasadena, California, United States. This picture of us taken to Father's Day, 1996. It shows my daughter, Katherine Rose [? Molderek, ?] with her boyfriend, Bob [? Bianchi, ?] and their two family dogs. It was taken in Oceanside, California, in-- oh, that I already said, yeah. This is my grandson, the son of my daughter, William Morris [? Snedeker, ?] III. And the picture was taken-- I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Maybe like 1994--

speaker: interviewer
text: And where?

speaker: interviewee
text: In California-- where, I don't know exactly. This throws the family of my son, Roger Frank Snyder, who is shown on the left side standing. Next to him is his wife, Linda Snyder. In front, from the left, is older daughter, Kyla; in the middle, son Ezra; and in front, the little Joy Snyder. All a very happy family. They are, at times-- at this time, living in Juneau, Alaska, where this picture was taken sometime in late 1995.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this is the last photograph. We're going to be leaving you shortly. Again, I want to thank you very much, Mr. Snyder, for sharing everything with us.

speaker: interviewee
text: I thank you very much. It was a privilege-- unexpected. And I'm very happy to provide the information. Anytime you want more, you can have [LAUGHS] more.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. You got in--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I-- I thought of--
